summary.one_line: Annambhaṭṭa's Tarka-Saṅgraha as the epistemological scaffold for Śāktopāya — the 16 padārthas, 4 pramāṇas, and the method of inference as the path of recognition.
summary.scope: Covers the complete primer of Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika epistemology with cross-tradition comparison to the pramāṇa theory of the Tantrāloka. Focus on the 16 padārthas, the 4 pramāṇas (pratyakṣa, anumāna, upamāna, śabda), the structure of inference (svārtha/parārtha), and apavarga as liberation-through-knowledge.

sources.label: Annambhaṭṭa — Tarka-Saṅgraha with Dīpikā, ed. S. Kuppuswami Sastri, 2nd ed. 1951

body.text: Gautama enumerates sixteen padārthas — means of valid knowledge (pramāṇa), objects of valid knowledge (prameya), doubt (saṃśaya), purpose (prayojana), instances (dṛṣṭānta), established conclusions (siddhānta), members of syllogism (avayava), reductio ad absurdum (tarka), decisive knowledge (nirṇaya), arguing for truth (vāda), arguing constructively as well as destructively for victory (jalpa), merely destructive argument (vitandā), fallacious reasons (hetvābhāsa), quibbling (chala), specious and unavailing objections (jāti), and vulnerable points (nigrahasthāna). These sixteen are not metaphysical categories in the Vaiśeṣika sense but sixteen topics which one should study to master the details of Nyāya dialectics. The Tarka-Saṅgraha, as a syncretist primer, reduces these to the seven Vaiśeṣika padārthas — substance (dravya), quality (guṇa), activity (karma), generality (sāmānya), particularity (viśeṣa), inherence (samavāya), and non-existence (abhāva).

The Naiyāyikas recognize four pramāṇas: perception (pratyakṣa), inference (anumāna), assimilation (upamāna), and verbal testimony (śabda). The Cārvākas recognize only perception; the Bauddhas and Vaiśeṣikas, perception and inference; the Sāṅkhyas add verbal testimony as a third; the Prabhākaras add presumptive testimony (arthāpatti) as a fifth; the Bhāṭṭas and Advaitins add non-cognition (anupalabdhi) as a sixth. The Nyāya position — four and only four — is argued to be the most parsimonious: arthāpatti reduces to anumāna, anupalabdhi to pratyakṣa of absent objects. This epistemological minimalism parallels the Tantrāloka's pramāṇa theory, where Abhinavagupta (TA I.66-74) similarly argues for the self-evidence of consciousness (prakāśa) as the sole ultimate pramāṇa, with the four empirical pramāṇas being its limited operations.

Inference (anumāna) is defined as the instrument of inferential knowledge (anumiti-karaṇam anumānam). Inferential knowledge is defined as knowledge arising from a consideration (parāmarśa) — specifically, the knowledge that 'This mountain possesses fire because it possesses smoke, and wherever there is smoke there is fire, as in a kitchen.' The three-term structure is: (1) vyāpti — the invariable concomitance ('Wherever there is smoke, there is fire'); (2) pakṣadharmatā — the presence of the probans in the subject ('The mountain possesses smoke'); (3) parāmarśa — the combined consideration ('The mountain, which possesses smoke that is pervaded by fire, possesses fire'). From this parāmarśa arises the inferential conclusion ('The mountain possesses fire'). This three-step movement from universal concomitance to particular application through a synthetic consideration is the exact logical structure of Śāktopāya: the recognition (pratyabhijñā) that the particular self (pramātṛ) is pervaded by universal Śiva-consciousness, established through the 'invariable concomitance' of the Āgamic revelation.

Inference is of two kinds: for oneself (svārthānumāna) and for others (parārthānumāna). Svārthānumāna is the purely mental process: observing the invariable concomitance between smoke and fire through repeated observation in a kitchen, then going to a mountain, seeing smoke, recollecting the concomitance, and generating the inferential cognition. Parārthānumāna is the five-limbed syllogism (pañcāvayava-vākya) used to communicate the inference to another: (1) pratijñā — 'The mountain possesses fire'; (2) hetu — 'Because it possesses smoke'; (3) udāharaṇa — 'Wherever there is smoke, there is fire, as in a kitchen'; (4) upanaya — 'The mountain is such (i.e., possesses smoke)'; (5) nigamana — 'Therefore, the mountain possesses fire.' This five-limbed structure is the discursive form of the same recognition: the teacher (guru) communicates the vyāpti through śabda-pramāṇa, enabling the student's own svārthānumāna. In Śāktopāya, the guru's upadeśa plays the role of the udāharaṇa — the 'example' that establishes the universal concomitance between Śiva-consciousness and one's own awareness.

The final cessation of all miseries (apavarga) is the goal of both the Vaiśeṣika and Nyāya systems. Apavarga is liberation-through-knowledge — not through ritual (karma) or devotion (bhakti), but through the tattva-jñāna (knowledge of the categories) that eliminates the false identification of the self (ātman) with body, senses, and mind. The Nyāya holds that mokṣa is the absolute cessation of all pain (duḥkhātyanta-viccheda), attained when the self is freed from all qualities (guṇas) through the destruction of the cycle of birth and rebirth. This is directly comparable to the Āṇavopāya's culmination: when the individual self (aṇu) exhausts all kalpanā (constructive thought) through correct reasoning (nyāya), it realizes its identity with Śiva. The difference is that Nyāya's apavarga is a negative state (absence of pain), while Tantrāloka's mokṣa is the positive realization of one's own bliss-consciousness (ānanda-cit). The Tarka-Saṅgraha provides the logical scaffolding: until the categories are correctly known, liberation is impossible.

The Naiyāyikas argue against recognizing śakti (potency) as a distinct category — counter-action consists merely in the presence of counter-agents, not in the destruction of some mysterious potency. Similarly, they argue that upamāna (assimilation) is distinct from both anumāna and śabda: it is the knowledge of similarity gained through the perception of resemblance, e.g., 'A gavaya is like a cow' — a unique epistemic operation that cannot be reduced to inference or testimony. This precision in demarcating the instruments of knowledge is the precondition for what the Tantrāloka calls Śāktopāya — the path of the empowered means, where the Śakti of recognition (pratyabhijñā-śakti) operates through the discrimination of the real from the unreal. Just as the Naiyāyika must carefully distinguish which pramāṇa applies in which epistemic situation, the practitioner of Śāktopāya must discern when the Self is manifesting through pratyakṣa (direct perception of Śiva), anumāna (inference of Śiva from the world), or śabda (Āgamic revelation).

gaps: Detailed comparison with Tantrāloka I.66-74 pramāṇa theory needed
More examples of how each syllogistic limb maps to Śāktopāya practice
Full comparison of apavarga with Tantrāloka's mokṣa doctrine needed

issues.title: Tantrāloka pramāṇa comparison incomplete
issues.description: TA I.66-74 presents Abhinavagupta's pramāṇa theory. A systematic verse-by-verse comparison with the Tarka-Saṅgraha's four-pramāṇa system is needed.

issues.title: Missing treatment of pratyakṣa as direct recognition
issues.description: The Tarka-Saṅgraha's treatment of pratyakṣa (perception) needs comparison with the Pratyabhijñā theory of direct recognition of Śiva.

versions.summary: Initial RO with 6 passages covering the 16 padārthas, the 4 pramāṇas, the structure of anumāna (svārtha/parārtha), apavarga, and the pramāṇa discrimination as Śāktopāya.

timeline.event: RO created in draft state with six passages from the Tarka-Saṅgraha
